content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
November 6,
1995
Kenneth A.
Smith
Nevada County Planning Commission
11490 Via Vista
Nevada City,
CA
95959
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—95—344
Dear Mr.
Smith:
This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
1
!
Since you do not have a specific decision pending before you
1
your letter is considered a request for informal assistance.2/
QUESTION
You are a member of the Nevada County Planning Commission.
May you receive expense reimbursement from the Nevada County Board
of Realtors for monitoring meetings of the Nevada County Board of
Supervisors?
3 /
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18995.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2!
Informal assistance does notprovide the reqilestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
It appears from your question that you may be asking if
attending these meetings and sitting as a planning commissioner
may be an incompatible activity.
If so, that question is not
addressed in the Political Reform Act.
The Office of the Attorney
General and the local district attorney resolve issues regarding
the incompatibility activities of public officials and the
doctrine of incompatible public offices.
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1—95—344
Page 2
CONCLUS ION
The Act does not prevent you from receiving such payments.
FACTS
You have recently been appointed to the Nevada County
Planning Commission (“planning commission”).
For the past several
years,
you have been attending Nevada County Board of Supervisor
meetings in order to write a report for the Nevada County Board of
Realtors legislative committee (“board of realtors”).
The board
of realtors pays you your expenses of approximately $50 to $100
per month for such activity.
You merely observe the board
meetings and do not testify or otherwise participate
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence. a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
A a member of the Nevada County
Planning Commission, you are a public official within the meaning
of the Act.
(Section 82048.)
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s iimnédiate
family, or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commerciallendlng
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.


Advice File No.
1—95-344
Page
3
(e)
Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
The amount of the value of
gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the commission in accordance
with the consumer price index.
4
!
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
(Section 87103.)
“Income” as used in Section 87103(c)
above means a payment
received,
including but not limited to any salary,
wage, advance,
dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift,
loan,
forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by the filer,
reimbursement for expenses or per diem.
(Section 82030.)
You indicated in your letter that you receive abóut $50 to
$100 per month for expenses from the board of realtors for
attending board of supervisor meetings and reporting back to its
legislative committee.
Thus, the board of realtors is a source of
income to you within the meaning of Section 87103(c)
above.
Additionally, assuming the board of realtors is a business entity,
if you are considered an employee of the board of realtors, your
position is an economic interest within the meaning of Section
87103 (d).
Accordingly,
you cannot make, participate in making,
or
use your official position to influence a decision which will have
a reasonably foreseeable
5
! material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the
board of realtors.
4’
Effective January 1,
1995, the value of a disqualifying gift
was raised to $280.
5/
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
1—95—344
Page 4
Material Financial Effect
If you determine that any decision you participate in will
affect the board of realtors, you must next determine if the
decision will have a “material financial effect” on the board of
realtors.
Commission Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.2
(enclosed)
define “material financial effect” on a business entity.
Regulation 18702.1 determines material financial effect when the
board of realtors is directly affected by decisions of the
planning commission.
The board of realtors is directly before the planning
commission when it initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal,
or similar request, or is a named
party in,
or the subject of, the proceeding.
A person or business
entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit,
or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person or business entity.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
For example, where the board of realtors is directly before
the planning commission,
as an applicant or the subject of the
decision, Regulation 18702.1(a) (1) provides that the effect of the
decision on a board of realtors is deemed material and you would
be prohibited from participating in that decision.
Regulation 18702.2 determine material financial effect when
the board of realtors would be indirectly affected by planning
commission decisions.
To determine if the board of realtors will
be materially affected by a decision,
you must first determine the
financial size of the board of realtors as a business entity.
6
!
You would next apply the specific subsection,
(a)
through (g),
which represents the financial size of the board of realtors.
If
the board of realtors would be affected by the applicable monetary
threshold set out in the appropriate section, you would not be
able to participate in that decision.
Since you have just recently been appointed to the planning
commission and are probably unfamiliar with your duties as a
public official, I have enclosed a copy of a pamphlet titled
“California’s Conflict of Interest Law for Public Officials.”
This pamphlet will help educate you on your duties as a public
official to disqualify yourself from participating on certain
6/
As noted earlier,
we are assuming that the Nevada County Board
of Realtors is a business entity.
If this is incorrect and they
are actually a nonprofit organization,
Commission Regulation
18702.5
(enclosed)
would be used to determine the material
financial effect of a decision on a nonprofit organization who is
a source of income.
Moreover, your position as an employee of the
board of realtors would not, standing alone, be the basis for a
conflict.
Positions with nonprofit entities are not positions
with business entities under Section 87103(d).


Advice File No.
1—95—344
Page
5
government decisions.
Also enclosed is a separate pamphlet titled
“Conflict of Interest and Campaign Contributions.”
Should you
consider running for an elected office, this pamphlet describes
when campaign contributions to you would be the basis for a
conflict while a member of the planning commission.
We suggest that when you have specific decisions which may
affect any of your economic interests, including the board of
realtors, that you contact the Commission’s Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901 for assistance.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Co
sel
By.
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC/JET/t
Enclosures

May you receive expense reimbursement from the Nevada County Board
of Realtors for monitoring meetings of the Nevada County Board of
Supervisors?
3 ;

Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)

428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

1—95—344

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prevent you from receiving such payments.
